definitions.canonical_meaning:
This document serves two simultaneous roles:

1) **Constitutional Specification (authoritative)**

2) **Backend Implementation Guide (actionable)**

The term `policy` is abandoned for the SiGMMa V2.3 authority architecture where it was previously used to describe the runtime-bound layer carrying organization-specific variable authority and adjudication inputs.

The correct term is:


This decision is binding.

This document defines the authoritative structural model for SiGMMa V2.3.

It establishes:

This structural model supersedes prior structural artifacts whose authority model assumed that constitution directly carried the full space of operative variable rules.

This artifact is binding.

SiGMMa is a constitutional system in which:


This document defines the authoritative structural model of the system. It governs all classification, design, implementation, and validation activities.

This artifact is binding.

The Translation Layer is the constitutional layer responsible for transforming constitutionally valid semantic state into downstream signals, representations, recommendations, and application-consumable outputs without acquiring independent authority.

It exists between constitutional meaning and downstream use.

It does not define semantic primitives.
It does not execute mutations.
It does not originate organizational authority.
It does not govern interface behavior.

Its function is to preserve fidelity while enabling use.

Downstream application consumption of constitutional meaning should ordinarily occur through constitutionally valid translation mechanisms. Direct downstream consumption of upstream state is permitted only where separately authorized by constitutional design and must not be used as an informal bypass around translation discipline.

Any authorized direct-consumption path must preserve the same distinction between canonical state and downstream projection that translation would otherwise enforce.

This artifact is binding.

definitions.term: CEK Audit Evidence Sufficiency and Provenance
definitions.canonical_meaning:
This artifact defines the constitutional rules governing audit evidence sufficiency and provenance within the CEK.

Its purpose is to ensure that:

A constitutionally relevant statement about system behavior, legality, ordering, outcome, state, replay validity, recovery validity, durability posture, classification identity, or evidentiary sufficiency.
The constitutionally governed category of an Audit Claim that determines the required evidence elements, sufficiency criteria, and admissibility constraints for that claim.
The set of constitutionally admitted records used to support an Audit Claim.
The condition in which the available evidence is adequate to fully support an Audit Claim without inference.
The condition in which an evidence source is constitutionally permitted to support an Audit Claim.
The traceable origin, integrity, and binding of evidence to canonical history and governing law.
Any data source that is not constitutionally allowed to support audit conclusions.
Any evidentiary element whose presence, closure status, ordering relation, or provenance is mandatory for a given Audit Claim Class.
The condition in which a Required Evidence Element is not required for a specific Audit Claim because governing law and reconstructable prior closure make that element inapplicable.
The ordered, coherent, non-contradictory chain of admissible evidence supporting one Audit Claim.

An Audit Claim asserting that a constitutionally significant element, event, decision, or historical unit is absent from canonical history, absent from a Trace Unit, absent from a required closure chain, or otherwise non-present in a governed scope.

definitions.term: CEK Boundary and Authority Enforcement Closure
definitions.canonical_meaning:
This artifact defines the constitutional closure rules by which authority boundaries are established, enforced, and kept non-permeable across the CEK and all adjacent surfaces.

Its purpose is to eliminate ambiguity concerning:


This artifact ensures that constitutional authority remains singular, bounded, and enforceable across the CEK system as a whole.
A domain constitutionally permitted to define law, represent runtime law, or produce constitutionally governing CEK decisions for the role assigned to it.

Any surface that may submit inputs, hold evidence, display outputs, provide operational support, or preserve subordinate records without possessing constitutional authority for the role under evaluation.
Any condition in which a Non-Authoritative Surface influences constitutional legality, classification, continuation, replay sufficiency, durability truth, or other governed outcome.
Any condition in which a Non-Authoritative Surface replaces, overrides, patches, or stands in for a constitutionally authoritative decision basis.
Any condition in which an Authoritative Domain is treated as having constitutional authority beyond the role explicitly granted by governing law.
The constitutional limit separating what a given domain may lawfully determine from what it may not determine.
The governed set of constraints by which non-authoritative influence is prevented, detected, rejected, and classified.

definitions.term: CEK Canonical Input and Ordering Closure
definitions.canonical_meaning:
This artifact defines the constitutional closure rules for canonical historical inputs and canonical ordering identity across persistence, audit, replay, checkpoint-assisted replay, and recovery.

Its purpose is to eliminate ambiguity concerning:


This artifact exists to ensure that canonical historical input identity and canonical ordering identity are singular, deterministic, replay-safe, and non-fragmented.
The constitutionally authoritative durable historical input surface from which replay and recovery determine canonical history.

The singular constitutionally authoritative ordering identity by which canonical historical sequence is determined wherever ordering is required for replay, recovery, persistence binding, or audit binding.

The constitutional rule that CPUs are the sole authoritative historical replay and recovery input substrate, with all other relevant artifacts subordinate, derivative, or evidentiary only unless another section expressly grants a tightly constrained non-substitutive role.
A field, reference, or record element that mirrors the Canonical Ordering Identity for local usability or evidentiary purposes without becoming an independent ordering authority.
The constitutionally admitted CPU interval that must be evaluated to satisfy replay law for the decision under consideration.
The CPU-governed interval whose replay sufficiency must be established before continuation may be authorized.
Any condition in which two or more artifacts can each be read, implemented, or treated as independently authoritative for the same historical-input role or the same ordering role.

definitions.term: CEK Cross-Artifact Consistency and Conflict Resolution
definitions.canonical_meaning:
This artifact defines the constitutional rules by which CEK artifacts operate as one coherent system and by which cross-artifact inconsistency is detected, classified, resolved where lawfully possible, or rejected where not lawfully resolvable.

Its purpose is to eliminate ambiguity concerning:


This artifact prevents locally coherent but systemically contradictory CEK behavior.

The exact governed scope within which CEK outputs are compared for consistency, including as applicable command identity, replay interval, recovery interval, authority scope, operation class, temporal law identity, and canonical historical interval.
A constitutional domain governed by one CEK artifact and capable of producing constitutionally significant outputs relevant to a governed Evaluation Context.

A constitutionally significant result produced by an Artifact Domain, including as applicable decision outcomes, classifications, postures, ordering determinations, trace sufficiency determinations, replay-validity determinations, evidence-sufficiency determinations, law-identity determinations, and atomicity determinations.
The set of all applicable Artifact Outputs for one governed Evaluation Context.
The condition in which all applicable Artifact Outputs for a governed Evaluation Context are mutually satisfiable under governing law and jointly support one lawful system interpretation.
A condition in which two or more applicable Artifact Outputs for the same governed Evaluation Context cannot be jointly satisfied under governing law.

A condition in which multiple Artifact Outputs appear to align, but that apparent alignment depends on inadmissible evidence, unresolved ambiguity, scope mismatch, law-identity mismatch, missing closure, or another prior constitutional defect.
An Artifact Domain whose output is constitutionally valid only if one or more prerequisite Artifact Domains have already produced admissible and sufficient outputs.
An explicit constitutional rule that determines how a specific type of Cross-Artifact Conflict is lawfully resolved.
A narrow constitutional rule assigning determinative priority to one Artifact Output over another for a defined conflict type, scope, and interval.
The single lawful CEK result for a governed Evaluation Context after admissibility, sufficiency, consistency, and conflict rules have been applied.

A governed comparison context in which historical execution outputs and replay, recovery, or audit outputs are compared for consistency across different evaluative modes while still referring to the same canonical interval and law-identity basis.

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] CEK Cross-Artifact Consistency and Conflict Resolution — Precedence Rules
definitions.canonical_meaning: a result derived under wrong or unresolved Law Identity MUST NOT take precedence over a result derived under the correct temporally applicable Law Identity

definitions.term: [MUST] CEK Cross-Artifact Consistency and Conflict Resolution — Failure Propagation and System Outcome
definitions.canonical_meaning: propagation MUST follow the governing failure-propagation posture for the affected role

This artifact defines the constitutional rule by which a constitutionally significant CEK decision becomes complete, evidentially closed, and lawfully usable for downstream constitutional progression.

Its purpose is to prevent:


This artifact binds into one governed closure model:


This artifact does not restate the substantive legality rules of other CEK artifacts. It governs the conditions under which their decisions are constitutionally complete and therefore admissible for downstream reliance.

The smallest constitutionally significant CEK determination that affects canonical progression, replay legality, recovery legality, durability posture, authority containment, or constitutional failure posture.
The unique governed gate, enforcement point, or constitutional evaluation boundary at which a Decision Event is determined.
The minimum constitutionally sufficient closure structure for one Decision Event. A DCU consists of the mandatory closure elements required by this artifact for the relevant decision class.
A Decision Event whose legality, admissibility, authorization, or equivalent governed determination is resolved from a lawfully bound IR package.
The constitutionally governed point at which a Decision Event becomes closure-complete for purposes of downstream constitutional reliance.

The condition in which all mandatory closure elements for a Decision Event are present, mutually bound as required, singular where singularity is required, and durably recoverable to the degree required by governing law.
The condition in which the ordered set of closure-complete Decision Events required for a Trace Unit is present, deterministic, non-contradictory, and durably reconstructable.

This artifact defines the constitutional isolation rules that prevent derivative surfaces from participating in canonical execution, runtime legality, replay authority, recovery authority, durability authority, continuation authority, or constitutional failure closure.

Its purpose is to ensure that:

Any artifact, state representation, storage surface, cached representation, indexed view, projection, checkpoint payload, mirror, or reconstructed view that is not itself canonical constitutional history.
An input surface constitutionally admitted to govern execution, replay, recovery, durability evaluation, continuation, or constitutional classification.
Any role in which a surface influences legality, authority, ordering, replay sufficiency, continuation authorization, durability status, or constitutional classification.
Any case in which a derivative surface affects a runtime governing role directly or indirectly.
The constitutional boundary separating admissible canonical inputs from inadmissible derivative inputs in CEK runtime behavior.

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] CEK Derivative Runtime Isolation — Execution-Time Isolation
definitions.canonical_meaning: derivative surfaces MUST NOT determine whether a classified failure is tolerable

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] CEK Derivative Runtime Isolation — Contradiction Rule
definitions.canonical_meaning: the derivative surface MUST NOT prevail

definitions.term: [MUST] CEK Derivative Runtime Isolation — Contradiction Rule
definitions.canonical_meaning: where the contradiction indicates possible authority leakage or derivative influence, the event MUST be classified accordingly

definitions.term: [MUST] CEK Derivative Runtime Isolation — Narrow Admission Rule
definitions.canonical_meaning: MUST remain non-substitutive

definitions.term: CEK Execution Atomicity and Partial Failure Handling
definitions.canonical_meaning:
This artifact defines the constitutional rules governing execution atomicity and the handling of partial failure within the CEK.

Its purpose is to ensure that:
The smallest constitutionally governed unit of mutation evaluated through the CEK gate chain.
An execution unit whose canonical effect is indivisible: it either produces a complete, lawful state transition or produces no canonical state change.
Any failure condition occurring after execution has begun but before atomic completion is achieved.
Any state representation that reflects partial application of a mutation unit prior to atomic completion.
The point beyond which canonical state must not reflect any part of a failed execution unit.

The constitutionally governed scope of the execution unit as determined before mutation effect and before durability completion. That boundary MUST NOT be repartitioned, narrowed, widened, or re-segmented by runtime convenience once evaluation has begun.

The constitutionally governed point at which an execution unit becomes admissible as canonically successful and therefore visible to downstream constitutional evaluation.
The constitutionally governed point at which an execution unit becomes admissible as canonically successful and therefore visible to downstream constitutional evaluation.
Any implementation-local, pre-canonical, speculative, staged, buffered, or coordination state used operationally during evaluation or persistence of an execution unit.
The condition in which the execution unit has lawfully crossed the Canonical Visibility Boundary and may therefore be treated as one complete canonical transition.

definitions.term: [MUST] CEK Execution Atomicity and Partial Failure Handling — V.0.B Non-Canonical Durable Residue Doctrine
definitions.canonical_meaning: MUST be treated as non-canonical

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] CEK Execution Atomicity and Partial Failure Handling — V.0.B Non-Canonical Durable Residue Doctrine
definitions.canonical_meaning: MUST NOT serve as recovery base

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] CEK Execution Atomicity and Partial Failure Handling — Multi-Step Mutation Units
definitions.canonical_meaning: partial internal progress MUST NOT be externally observable

This artifact defines the constitutional rules by which CEK execution, replay, recovery, classification, and closure remain deterministic and reproducible.

Its purpose is to eliminate ambiguity concerning:


This artifact ensures that the CEK is not merely intended to behave consistently, but is constitutionally required to produce identical governed results from identical admitted conditions.
The condition in which identical constitutionally admitted inputs, ordering, governing law identity, and admissible evidence yield identical constitutionally significant outcomes.
The condition in which a constitutionally significant CEK process can be re-executed or re-evaluated under constitutionally equivalent admitted conditions and produce the same governed result.
The complete set of inputs, identities, ordering anchors, governing law identities, and admissible evidence permitted by governing law to influence the result under evaluation.
Any non-admitted condition, implementation behavior, or environment-dependent factor that can influence a constitutionally significant CEK outcome.
The constitutional limit separating admitted governing inputs from prohibited influences that would create nondeterminism.
Any condition in which constitutionally significant CEK results cannot be reproduced from the same constitutionally admitted condition set.

Define the explicit, ordered, non-bypassable execution gates of the CEK. These gates are the only lawful pathway for mutation, commit progression, durability acknowledgment, replay, and recovery continuation.

Every gate is:

No execution path may skip, reorder, or partially apply these gates.
MUST use the IR version applicable to the interval OR a constitutionally proven equivalent migration

definitions.term: [MUST] CEK Execution Gates Specification — Atomicity and Concurrency
definitions.canonical_meaning: Concurrent evaluations MUST preserve determinism (no race-dependent outcomes).

definitions.term: [MUST] CEK Execution Gates Specification — Replay and Recovery Integration
definitions.canonical_meaning: DIG MUST be enforced during replay and recovery.

definitions.term: CEK Execution Trace and Audit Binding
definitions.canonical_meaning:
This artifact defines the constitutional execution trace model of the CEK and binds that model to audit, replay, IR binding, gate decisions, failure classification, and canonical progression.

Its purpose is to ensure that CEK execution is not only lawful, but reconstructable, auditable, and replay-verifiable at the level of decision path rather than outcome alone.

It ensures that:
The ordered, deterministic constitutional record of CEK decision flow for an execution unit, replay interval, or recovery interval.

The smallest constitutionally governed trace scope requiring ordered decision reconstruction. A Trace Unit may correspond to a command, replay interval, recovery interval, or other explicitly constitutionalized CEK process.
A single constitutionally significant decision or stateful progression point within a Trace Unit.
The constitutionally governed sequence of Trace Events within a Trace Unit.
The constitutionally identifiable point at which a Trace Unit becomes complete, either by lawful successful progression or by failure closure.

The condition in which replay or re-evaluation produces the same constitutionally required Trace Events in the same required order with the same governing identities, primary classes, and primary consequence postures.
The durable representation of a Trace Event.

definitions.term: [MUST] CEK Execution Trace and Audit Binding — Operative Principle
definitions.canonical_meaning: every constitutionally significant decision MUST emit a Trace Record

definitions.term: [MUST] CEK Execution Trace and Audit Binding — Ordering Requirements
definitions.canonical_meaning: Trace Events MUST be emitted in constitutionally governed order

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] CEK Execution Trace and Audit Binding — Ordering Requirements
definitions.canonical_meaning: order MUST NOT depend on runtime scheduling, thread interleaving, environment, buffering strategy, or logging configuration

This artifact defines the CEK-wide closure rule for constitutional failure classification.

Its purpose is to ensure that every failed gate, blocked transition, rejected execution, rejected replay, rejected continuation, durability fault, uncertainty condition, authority violation, integrity failure, inadmissible runtime condition, and taxonomy defect terminates in an explicit constitutional failure class.

No CEK failure may remain silent, private, implicit, implementation-local, semantically unclassified, consequence-undetermined, or precedence-undetermined.

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] CEK Failure Classification Closure — XII.2 Posture Compatibility Rule
definitions.canonical_meaning: replay-halting classes MUST NOT map to warning-only outcomes

definitions.term: CEK Failure Propagation and System Response
definitions.canonical_meaning:
This artifact defines the constitutional rules by which classified CEK failures propagate, constrain downstream behavior, and produce constitutionally complete system response.

Its purpose is to eliminate ambiguity concerning:


This artifact ensures that constitutional failures do not merely exist as classified records, but govern downstream CEK behavior deterministically and within lawful propagation boundaries.
The governed extension of a classified failure’s constitutional effect into downstream CEK decisions, stages, states, or artifact usability boundaries.
Any CEK stage, process, state, artifact, or authorization boundary whose behavior is constitutionally constrained by a classified failure.
The constitutional limit beyond which a given failure posture must not extend, and within which it must be enforced.
The condition in which the required system response to a classified failure is fully determined and enforced to the degree required by governing law.
Multiple classified failures whose required downstream effects can be jointly enforced without contradiction.

A condition in which two apparent downstream response interpretations would produce mutually inconsistent constitutional behavior and therefore require resolution by governing posture and precedence law.

definitions.term: [MUST] CEK Failure Propagation and System Response — VII.1 Reject_And_Stop
heading_path: Propagation Rules by Primary Consequence Posture
definitions.canonical_meaning: the current progression attempt MUST terminate as rejected

definitions.term: [MUST] CEK Failure Propagation and System Response — VII.2 Halt_And_Fault
heading_path: Propagation Rules by Primary Consequence Posture
definitions.canonical_meaning: the affected path or system state MUST enter the relevant faulted condition where governing law requires such state

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] CEK Failure Propagation and System Response — VII.3 Block_Continuation
heading_path: Propagation Rules by Primary Consequence Posture
definitions.canonical_meaning: continuation authorization MUST NOT be granted

definitions.term: [MUST] CEK Failure Propagation and System Response — VII.4 Enter_Uncertain_State
heading_path: Propagation Rules by Primary Consequence Posture
definitions.canonical_meaning: the affected state, interval, or determination MUST remain explicitly uncertain

definitions.term: [MUST] CEK Failure Propagation and System Response — VII.5 Invalidate_Derivative_Artifact
heading_path: Propagation Rules by Primary Consequence Posture
definitions.canonical_meaning: the affected derivative artifact MUST be treated as unusable for any governed role prohibited by the invalidation

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] CEK Failure Propagation and System Response — VII.5 Invalidate_Derivative_Artifact
heading_path: Propagation Rules by Primary Consequence Posture
definitions.canonical_meaning: invalidation of a derivative artifact MUST NOT be mistaken for invalidation of canonical history unless another governing artifact explicitly requires such effect

definitions.term: [MUST] CEK Failure Propagation and System Response — VII.6 Escalate_To_Audit
heading_path: Propagation Rules by Primary Consequence Posture
definitions.canonical_meaning: the relevant failure and response state MUST be durably surfaced for audit-bound handling where governing law requires it

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] CEK Failure Propagation and System Response — VII.6 Escalate_To_Audit
heading_path: Propagation Rules by Primary Consequence Posture
definitions.canonical_meaning: audit escalation MUST NOT erase any blocking, halting, invalidating, or uncertainty-preserving effect otherwise required

This artifact verifies that system-layer invariants have explicit CEK enforcement loci and that each such locus is:


This artifact is defect-led. It does not restate governing law except where needed to isolate an enforcement defect.

definitions.term: CEK IR Admission and Equivalence Governance
definitions.canonical_meaning:
This artifact defines the constitutional rules by which IR packages become admissible for CEK use and by which IR equivalence claims are governed, proven, accepted, or rejected.

Its purpose is to eliminate ambiguity concerning:


This artifact ensures that IR authority is not treated as self-justifying merely because an IR package exists, loads successfully, compiles successfully, or appears semantically close to another package.
The governed constitutional decision that an IR package is admissible for a specific role, scope, class, and interval.
An IR package proposed for constitutional use before admission is decided.
The set of constitutionally required identities, proofs, boundaries, completeness conditions, and admissibility conditions supporting an IR Admission decision.
A claim that one IR package may lawfully stand in for another for a defined role, scope, class, and interval.
The constitutionally governed proof that a migrated or alternate IR package preserves the required legality, rejection, constraint, authority, and execution consequences for the exact admitted scope.
The exact IR package identity originally governing a historical interval.
A migrated IR package lawfully admitted as replay-capable substitute for a Historical IR Identity within a strictly bounded equivalence scope.
The exact role, authority scope, operation class, and effective interval for which IR Admission is decided.
The exact role, authority scope, operation class, and effective interval for which an IR Equivalence Claim is proven or rejected.

This artifact defines the only lawful mechanism by which the CEK binds execution and replay to IR as the sole executable representation of constitutional law.

It governs:

This artifact does not define constitutional source law, IR schema, or compiler internals except where those directly constrain runtime admissibility, historical replay legality, or binding evidence sufficiency.
The complete runtime-admissible representation of executable constitutional law for a defined authority scope, operation class, and effective governance interval.
The immutable identifier of a specific IR package.
The governed integrity evidence proving that the package loaded at runtime is exactly the package identified by the IRPID.
The governed act of associating an execution or replay interval with one specific admissible IR package.
The binding established before runtime legality evaluation of a command and before any mutation effect.
The binding established before replay begins for the relevant historical interval.
The constitutionally required conditions under which an IR package may serve as runtime law.
The deterministic, constitutionally governed selection surface from which admissible IR packages are resolved for execution and replay.
The durable evidentiary record proving which IR package governed a command, replay interval, or recovery-relevant legality decision.

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] CEK IR Runtime Binding Enforcement — Failure Handling
definitions.canonical_meaning: recovery continuation dependent on that replay MUST NOT be authorized

definitions.term: [MUST] CEK IR Runtime Binding Enforcement — Failure Handling
definitions.canonical_meaning: the failure MUST be classified and durably recorded

This artifact defines the constitutional rules that prevent any interface from escaping, circumventing, weakening, fragmenting, or partially substituting for the governed CEK execution chain.

Its purpose is to ensure that:

definitions.term: CEK Replay Equivalence and Validation Closure
definitions.canonical_meaning:
This artifact defines the constitutional rules by which replay outcomes are evaluated for equivalence and by which replay-validity decisions become constitutionally complete.

Its purpose is to eliminate ambiguity concerning:


This artifact ensures that replay determinism is not merely asserted, but validated against explicit constitutional equivalence criteria.
A constitutionally governed re-evaluation of a lawful CPU interval under replay law.
The authoritative CPU-governed historical interval against which replay is evaluated.
The constitutionally significant decision that determines whether a Replay Instance is equivalent, non-equivalent, or constitutionally non-validatable under governing law.
The ordered set of constitutionally significant replay-produced decision outcomes and required evidentiary bindings relevant to replay validation for the interval under evaluation.
A constitutionally governed rule specifying whether a replay element must match exactly, may vary only in explicitly subordinate non-governing ways, or is outside required equivalence scope.
Any detected difference between the Replay Instance and the Reference Historical Interval that violates a required Equivalence Criterion.
The condition in which the Replay Instance satisfies all mandatory Equivalence Criteria for the interval under evaluation.
The condition in which one or more mandatory Equivalence Criteria fail.

The condition in which replay validity cannot be lawfully established because required evidence, required ordering, required law identity, or required closure proof is missing, unreadable, ambiguous, or otherwise insufficient.

This artifact defines the constitutional rules by which CEK system completeness is asserted, proven, rejected, or withheld.

Its purpose is to eliminate ambiguity concerning:


This artifact does not introduce new behavior. It governs the constitutional conditions under which the CEK may lawfully be treated as a closed system.
The condition in which the CEK fully governs all admissible Evaluation Contexts and governed surfaces within scope without contradiction, omission, unresolved dependency, or undefined behavior.
The constitutional claim that the CEK satisfies System Completeness.
The full set of ratified artifacts, dependency relations, proof requirements, and cross-artifact validations required to support a lawful Completeness Assertion.

Any constitutional surface whose behavior must be determined by CEK law, including mutation admission, legality evaluation, commit and durability progression, replay, recovery, audit proof, trace reconstruction, law identity resolution, conflict resolution, and failure closure.
Any admissible state-affecting or constitutionally relevant path through a Governed Surface that is not fully constrained by CEK artifacts.
Any system behavior whose legality, outcome, constraint set, failure posture, or evidentiary requirements are not determined by constitutional rules.
A condition in which a path lacks constitutional governance but the gap remains undiscovered merely because the path has not yet been exercised.
The condition in which one artifact or domain appears internally complete without proving that the CEK as a whole is complete.

definitions.term: CEK Temporal Law Identity and Evaluation Context
definitions.canonical_meaning:
This artifact defines the constitutional rules governing temporal law identity and evaluation context within the CEK.

Its purpose is to ensure that:
The uniquely addressable, immutable, replay-reconstructable representation of governing constitutional rules applicable to an evaluation.
The admitted IR package or constitutionally equivalent law representation that carries a specific Law Identity for a defined authority scope, operation class, and effective interval.
The association between an event, interval, or evaluation and the Law Identity governing its evaluation.
The constitutionally governed temporal interval for which a Law Identity is applicable.
A constitutionally recognized change in governing law.
Evaluation of an event that has already occurred in canonical history.
The deterministic constitutional procedure by which the CEK resolves exactly one applicable Law Identity or rejects evaluation.
A condition in which more than one Law Identity is constitutionally eligible for the same evaluation and governing law does not resolve the conflict to exactly one result.
A condition in which no admissible Law Identity covers an evaluation that requires law binding.
Any condition in which different parts, stages, constraints, or consequences of one evaluation are drawn from different Law Identities without explicit constitutional authorization.

This artifact defines the constitutional rules by which CEK trace is proven complete, gap-free, causally sufficient, and reconstructable for every constitutionally significant execution, replay, recovery, classification, and closure path within scope.

Its purpose is to eliminate ambiguity concerning:


This artifact ensures that CEK trace is not treated as a useful diagnostic surface only. It is a constitutional sufficiency surface for causal reconstruction of governed decision flow.

The condition in which all constitutionally required Trace Events, ordering relations, bindings, classifications, and closure elements for a Trace Unit are present, singular where singularity is required, non-contradictory, durably recoverable, and sufficient to reconstruct the governed decision path without inference.

The condition in which the constitutionally significant decision flow of a Trace Unit can be rebuilt from constitutionally admitted evidence as one ordered, causally coherent, closure-complete path or lawfully classified set of paths where governing law explicitly admits branching.

Any Trace Event, ordering relation, identity, reference, binding, classification, posture, or closure element whose presence is constitutionally mandatory for reconstructing a Trace Unit within scope.

The condition in which trace evidence is adequate to support the constitutional role for which it is relied upon, including audit explanation, replay-path comparison, recovery explanation, and failure-path reconstruction.
Any missing Required Trace Element or missing required ordering relation that prevents closure-complete reconstruction of the governed path.

Any condition in which available trace evidence permits more than one materially different reconstruction of a constitutionally significant decision path and governing law does not resolve that multiplicity deterministically.
Any condition in which trace evidence asserts mutually inconsistent outcomes, orderings, bindings, classifications, or closure identities for the same required constitutional role.
A Trace Record or subset of Trace Records that exists without sufficient constitutionally bound linkage to form a reconstructable Trace Unit.
The complete constitutionally admitted evidentiary set from which a Trace Unit may be reconstructed.

definitions.term: [MUST] CEK Trace Completeness and Reconstructability — XI.8 Fragment and Interruption Handling
definitions.canonical_meaning: MUST remain evidentially subordinate

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] CEK Trace Completeness and Reconstructability — XI.8 Fragment and Interruption Handling
definitions.canonical_meaning: MUST NOT be treated as closure-complete historical path evidence

definitions.term: [MUST] CEK Trace Completeness and Reconstructability — XI.9 Evidentiary Stratification
definitions.canonical_meaning: MUST remain evidentially subordinate

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] CEK Trace Completeness and Reconstructability — XI.9 Evidentiary Stratification
definitions.canonical_meaning: MUST NOT be merged with later heuristic or operator-supplied material to simulate historical completeness

definitions.term: [MUST] CEK Trace Completeness and Reconstructability — XV.0 Temporal Governance of Completeness
definitions.canonical_meaning: failure-closing paths MUST include classified failure closure in trace

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] CEK Trace Completeness and Reconstructability — XV.0 Temporal Governance of Completeness
definitions.canonical_meaning: remediation tooling MUST NOT synthesize substitute historical trace as constitutional evidence

Define the constitutional rules governing ordering, concurrency, and scheduling of command execution such that determinism, replay equivalence, canonical ordering, and law-constrained execution are preserved.
The set of Execution Units that are admissible for scheduling within an Evaluation Context after all gating, dependency, and law constraints are satisfied.
The set of constraints determining ordering, including dependencies, law identity, canonical ordering rules, and execution gates.
The authoritative total order of Execution Units recorded in CPUs and used for replay and audit.

The complete constitutionally admitted set of inputs from which Canonical Order is resolved, including dependency relations, Evaluation Context identity, governing Law Identity, and any explicitly admitted ordering keys.

A subset of the Eligible Execution Set permitted to execute without violating dependency, atomicity, or law identity constraints.
A subset of the Eligible Execution Set permitted to execute without violating dependency, atomicity, or law identity constraints.
A constitutionally required constraint that forces sequential execution of Execution Units to preserve correctness.

definitions.term: Section VII — Transaction and Commit Governance Constitution
definitions.canonical_meaning: Define the constitutional rules governing transaction formation, commit eligibility, commit boundary integrity, commit proof, and partial-resolution admissibility such that canonical mutation becomes durable only through lawful, deterministic, and replay-safe commit.
The constitutionally governed commit candidate formed from one mutation unit or one constitutionally admitted set of mutation effects that must be evaluated together for lawful commit.
The exact constitutional scope of mutation effects, dependencies, and governed consequences that are evaluated as one Transaction Unit for commit.
The portion of a Transaction Unit proposed for commit.
The condition in which a Transaction Unit or lawfully admitted Commit Scope satisfies all constitutional requirements to cross the commit boundary.
The minimum constitutionally sufficient durable evidence that a Transaction Unit lawfully achieved commit eligibility and crossed the governed commit boundary.

A commit in which less than the full original candidate mutation scope is admitted for commit because governing law permits a semantically independent subset to commit while excluded scope remains blocked, rejected, or unresolved.

A scope that can be committed without changing the legality, meaning, ordering, replayability, or correctness of excluded scope and without depending directly or transitively on excluded unresolved scope.

The condition in which the Transaction Boundary remains explicit, stable, non-ambiguous, and not reshaped by implementation convenience after commit evaluation begins except where governing law explicitly permits bounded partial-resolution handling.
The immutable ordering identifier assigned to a committed Transaction Unit to anchor canonical order for persistence, replay, and audit.

definitions.term: Section X — Connector and Ingestion Governance Constitution
definitions.canonical_meaning: Define the constitutional rules governing connectors and ingestion such that all external inputs are transformed into unambiguous, fully classified, law-bound Intermediate Representation (IR) prior to CEK execution, with zero authority leakage and zero mutation bypass.
A non-authoritative interface that acquires external input and submits it to the ingestion pipeline.
The governed process that transforms external input into constitutionally admissible IR.
The machine-enforceable, fully classified, law-bound representation of input that CEK consumes.
The constitutionally governed boundary at which a constructed IR package becomes admissible for downstream CEK reliance after successful ingestion validation.
The explicit assignment of each input element into one or more constitutional rule types (e.g., invariant, prohibition, obligation, condition, definition, derivation).

The condition in which every admissible input element has been exhaustively classified and no required rule-bearing content remains unclassified, silently omitted, or deferred for downstream interpretation.
Any input condition where multiple interpretations, rule classifications, bindings, meanings, or admissible constructions are possible under governing law.

The reconstructable relation between source input elements and resulting IR elements, including lawful omission, rejection, decomposition, or aggregation where governing law explicitly permits such treatment.

Define the constitutional rules governing overrides such that no override may bypass constitutional authority, alter governing law, weaken replay or audit integrity, or transform prohibited behavior into lawful behavior by discretion, urgency, or operational convenience.

Its purpose is to ensure that:

A constitutionally governed temporary exception mechanism that modifies the handling of a lawfully identified condition only where another constitutional artifact explicitly authorizes such modification for a defined role, scope, and interval.
The explicit constitutional authority admitting an Override for a defined role, scope, and interval.
The exact bounded domain, object set, operation class, and interval to which an Override applies.
The minimum constitutionally sufficient evidence proving that an Override was lawfully invoked, within scope, under authorized conditions, and without violating override prohibitions.
The explicit temporal interval during which an Override is constitutionally effective.
The governed termination of Override effect at the end of the Override Interval or upon satisfaction of the terminating condition defined by governing law.
Any condition in which Override is used to conceal illegality, bypass failure closure, suppress missing prerequisites, or normalize what governing law would otherwise reject, block, or invalidate.
Any lasting system condition, state, interpretation, or behavior that remains after Override Expiration without explicit constitutional authority.

This artifact belongs to the Constitutionalization Framework layer.

It governs artifact form, artifact classification, contamination handling, and canonical admissibility across all constitutional production.

It does not belong in core because it does not define universal possibility structure.

It does not belong in any Constitutional Organization because it does not define organization-specific governance.

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] Governance Binding Refactor Impact Map — 9. Authority Model
definitions.canonical_meaning: governance bindings may instantiate admitted semantics but MUST NOT create new semantics, authority classes, command classes, or decision logic

definitions.term: Organyz Error Taxonomy — Freeze Audit Record
definitions.canonical_meaning: This audit validates the completeness, consistency, and enforceability of the Organyz error taxonomy across:

The Critique Coverage Record (CCR) defines the authoritative process artifact by which the system proves that critique, hardening, exhaustion testing, and closure validation have actually occurred for a constitutional artifact.

It exists to prevent false completion, invisible shortcutting, unproven closure, and narrative assertions of quality without traceable coverage.

No artifact may be declared exhausted, canonical, or closure-ready without CCR evidence.

The Closure Ledger is the constitutional mechanism that determines lawful closure of an artifact.

Closure is a state transition that must be:


Closure cannot be inferred from completeness, stability, or confidence. It must be explicitly adjudicated.

No artifact may be treated as closed, canonical, frozen, or binding without a valid Closure Ledger.

This document defines the production system by which semantic constitutional material is authored, normalized, compiled, validated, promoted, and admitted for CEK ingestion.

The purpose of the framework is not to improve one artifact in isolation. Its purpose is to make large-scale constitutional production operationally safe when the corpus contains hundreds of artifacts whose meaning must remain coherent across time, across layers, and across implementation boundaries.

This framework therefore governs the full path from human-authored constitutional meaning to machine-ingestible constitutional IR. It also governs the corpus as a whole, because file-local correctness is insufficient if corpus-level terminology, authority, dependency, or rule semantics drift.

This protocol defines the mandatory, non-discretionary process by which any constitutional artifact is created, critiqued, hardened, validated, and promoted to canonical status.

Its purpose is to guarantee that:


This protocol governs the build process itself. It is binding.

This protocol is strictly limited to one function:

Extraction of behavioral meaning from UI/UX material into constitutionally valid form.

It does not govern UI/UX design.

It does not define interface standards.

It does not prescribe user experience principles.

It does not regulate presentation quality.

Its sole purpose is to:


This protocol is an ingestion and translation boundary only.

Any attempt to use this protocol to define, constrain, or standardize UI/UX design itself is a category error and must be rejected.

This protocol is binding wherever design-originated material is introduced into the constitutional system.

definitions.term: AI Use and Application Specification - Engineering Appendix (V2)
definitions.canonical_meaning:
This appendix exists because the primary specification exceeded practical canvas length for reliable drafting, critique, and implementation hardening.

Its role is to carry the engineering-contract material required for backend and AI implementation while preserving the primary specification as the shorter constitutional authority artifact.

Relationship:

definitions.term: AI Use and Application Specification (V2 - Governance Binding Model)
definitions.canonical_meaning:
This document serves two simultaneous roles:

1) Constitutional Specification (authoritative)

2) Backend Implementation Guide (actionable)

definitions.term: Constitutional Execution — From Text to Machine-Readable State
definitions.canonical_meaning:
This document provides an implementation-oriented explanation of how a constitution is represented as machine-readable state and how that representation is used within the system. It is intended for engineering, product, and design teams to support consistent understanding of the system’s operation.

Its purpose is to reduce ambiguity in how canonical constitutional artifacts are interpreted and applied during implementation. It does not define, modify, or override constitutional behavior. All binding authority remains exclusively within the canonical constitutional artifacts, including the compiler specification, intermediate representation (IR), constitutional execution kernel (CEK), verification system, and enforcement bridge.

This document is explanatory only. It must not be used as a source of authority for system behavior, enforcement decisions, or implementation correctness. Where discrepancies arise, canonical artifacts are definitive and this document must be disregarded.
Defines the canonical aggregate state machine model governing identity, state, transitions, invariants, concurrency, commit authority, replay, and creation semantics.

This doctrine defines the strategic identity, governing interpretation rules, and category discipline for Organyz.

Its function is to prevent strategic drift by establishing what Organyz is, what problem it actually solves, what architectural conditions make that possible, how adjacent systems must be interpreted, and what the company must not claim or become.

This is a binding bridge-layer doctrine. It governs strategic interpretation, product framing, market language, and internal alignment. It does not replace constitutional core law, but it must remain subordinate to and consistent with core constitutional authority.

The Audit Lineage and Traceability Schema defines the authoritative structure through which the system represents traceable constitutional history across command processing, adjudication, execution, mutation, commit, durability, replay eligibility, and audit inspection.

It establishes the formal lineage model that binds:


No authoritative audit lineage exists outside this schema.

This specification defines the authority model governing actors, roles, permissions, denials, overrides, escalations, and authority resolution across SiGMMa V2.3.

It establishes:

This artifact is binding for all command validation and organizational governance.

This document defines the canonical, durable, and authoritative record produced for every command processed through the constitutional pipeline.

This record is the sole authoritative persistence artifact for command processing outcomes.

It MUST be sufficient to:


No alternate or partial representation is authoritative.

definitions.term: [MUST] Command Execution Record Schema — Non-Negotiable Invariants
definitions.canonical_meaning: the record MUST be immutable once finalized

definitions.term: [MUST] Command Execution Record Schema — Execution Outcome
definitions.canonical_meaning: approved commands MUST produce either executed or failed_after_approval

definitions.term: [MUST] Command Execution Record Schema — State Change References
definitions.canonical_meaning: if execution_outcome = executed or failed_after_approval, item_count MUST equal the full set of authoritative state changes produced

definitions.term: [MUST] Command Execution Record Schema — Emitted Event References
definitions.canonical_meaning: item_count MUST equal the full set of authoritative emitted events for this execution record

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] Command Execution Record Schema — Immutability and Finalization
definitions.canonical_meaning: the record MUST NOT be modified

This schema defines the only lawful mutation envelope.

No mutation exists outside a conformant command.

definitions.term: [MUST] Command Schema Definition — Payload Schema Binding and Precedence
definitions.canonical_meaning: payload MUST conform structurally

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] Command Schema Definition — Payload Schema Binding and Precedence
definitions.canonical_meaning: payload semantics MUST NOT override operation_type semantics

definitions.term: [MUST] Command Schema Definition — Canonical Command Envelope
definitions.canonical_meaning: Arrays MUST satisfy stated minimum cardinality.

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] Command Schema Definition — Canonical Command Envelope
definitions.canonical_meaning: Omission of optional fields MUST NOT change legality implicitly.

definitions.term: [MUST] Command Schema Definition — Payload Schema Version Compatibility
definitions.canonical_meaning: command MUST be rejected

This document defines the mandatory pipeline by which every command is received, normalized, validated, adjudicated, and either rejected, approved, executed, superseded, or failed.

No command may bypass this pipeline.

No implementation-local shortcut, direct execution path, administrative override path, or convenience handler may produce a mutation effect outside the pipeline defined here.

This pipeline is the constitutional enforcement sequence for command legality.

The Commit and Persistence Boundary defines the sole constitutional transition point at which validated state mutation becomes durable, immutable, authoritative system history.

It establishes:


No state becomes durable outside this boundary.

The Decision System SHALL govern all mutation-bearing system decisions from command intake through canonical commit eligibility handoff.

The Decision System exists to:


The Decision System SHALL NOT independently determine legality outside IR-governed evaluation.
Decision outcomes SHALL be deterministic for the same canonical inputs and IR binding.

definitions.term: [SHALL NOT] Decision System Constitution — Core Invariants
definitions.canonical_meaning: The Decision System SHALL NOT introduce independent rule interpretation.

definitions.term: Decision System Structural Specification — SiGMMa V2
definitions.canonical_meaning:
This document defines the structural composition of the Decision System within SiGMMa V2.

The Decision System is the sole legality gate of the constitutional system. This document maps its internal structure, validation order, dependency boundaries, rejection behavior, and output contract.

This document is structural only. It does not introduce new operational law, expand behavioral scope, or define implementation detail beyond what is required for structural clarity and authority enforcement.

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] Decision System Structural Specification — SiGMMa V2 — Internal Structural Decomposition
definitions.canonical_meaning: Unit 1 and Unit 2 MUST NOT be collapsed into a single ambiguous validation stage if that collapse obscures these distinct structural functions

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] Decision System Structural Specification — SiGMMa V2 — Deterministic Validation Pipeline
definitions.canonical_meaning: a rejected candidate command MUST NOT be transformed, repaired, or re-entered within the same evaluation pass

This document defines the canonical error taxonomy and its integration across the command validation and adjudication pipeline.

Errors are not incidental outputs. They are authoritative constitutional classifications of failure conditions.

No failure may be emitted without classification under this taxonomy.

definitions.term: [MUST] Error Taxonomy Integration Specification — Error Multiplicity
definitions.canonical_meaning: all MUST be emitted

definitions.term: [MUST] Error Taxonomy Integration Specification — Taxonomy Versioning and Compatibility
definitions.canonical_meaning: compatibility across taxonomy versions MUST be explicitly defined by constitutional artifact

The Error Taxonomy System defines the complete, deterministic, and machine-enforceable classification of all constitutional failures, rejections, and non-success outcomes across the system.

It governs how failures are:


No failure with constitutional effect may exist outside this taxonomy.
Every failure SHALL resolve to exactly one primary error classification.

definitions.term: [SHALL NOT] Error Taxonomy System — Core Invariants
definitions.canonical_meaning: Error classification SHALL NOT introduce new authority or interpretation.
CEK SHALL terminate execution when error is blocking

This document defines the canonical structure and semantics of events emitted by the execution system.

Events are immutable, ordered, and authoritative records of observable consequences produced during execution.

Events are distinct from:


Events capture externally and internally observable occurrences derived from execution.
every emitted event MUST be traceable to a command execution record

definitions.term: [MUST] Event Schema Definition — Event Identity and Versioning
definitions.canonical_meaning: `event_id` MUST be globally unique and immutable
ordering MUST match deterministic execution order
payload MUST be structurally valid

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] Event Schema Definition — Payload Structure
definitions.canonical_meaning: payload MUST NOT alter event classification

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] Event Schema Definition — Event Immutability
definitions.canonical_meaning: event records MUST NOT be modified

This document defines the highest-order constitutional macro structure of the SiGMMa V2 system. Its function is to establish the total constitutional universe within which all subordinate constitutional artifacts must be placed, interpreted, validated, extended, and enforced. It defines layer architecture, domain taxonomy, authority origin and flow, dependency direction, overlay topology, structural prohibitions, legitimacy boundaries, lifecycle domains, and vertical extension boundaries.

This document is authoritative on macro-structural placement and constitutional relationship. It is not authoritative on subsystem behavior. It does not implement compiler behavior, define intermediate representation schema, restate constitutional execution kernel logic, restate verification execution mechanics, or define application-specific behavior. Those matters are governed only by their proper downstream constitutional artifacts.

The purpose of this document is to eliminate ambiguity about where constitutional meaning originates, where each class of rule belongs, how structural dependency may flow, how extension may occur, and what forms of cross-layer contamination are constitutionally prohibited.

definitions.term: SiGMMa V2 - Interaction Event Substrate Specification
definitions.canonical_meaning:
This specification defines the **Interaction Event Substrate** for the UI Generation System.

This artifact establishes the system-layer structural substrate required for governed UI interaction without introducing action meaning, business semantics, workflow intent, command selection logic, or domain consequence into the system layer.

It exists to define the constitutional rules for how an interaction-capable generated UI surface may represent, identify, emit, bind, trace, validate, and hand off interaction signals to downstream constitutional layers.

This artifact does not authorize the UI to decide what an interaction means. It defines the signal substrate only.

The IR Runtime Binding Specification defines the authoritative mechanism by which machine-evaluable constitutional structures are bound to runtime execution within the Constitutional Execution Kernel (CEK).

It establishes:


IR is not advisory. IR is the sole machine-evaluable execution form admissible at runtime.

Defines the deterministic emission of identity, lineage, structural traceability, binding references, and recoverability metadata for generated UI output.

This artifact exists to ensure generated UI output remains traceable, replay-stable, structurally auditable, and reconstruction-sufficient.

Metadata emission is part of the constitutional output surface.
Every emitted UI node SHALL carry a complete metadata payload.

definitions.term: [SHALL NOT] Metadata Emission Specification — Core Invariants
definitions.canonical_meaning: Metadata SHALL NOT encode behavioral or authority semantics.

definitions.term: [MUST] Metadata Emission Specification — Node Identity Metadata
definitions.canonical_meaning: node identity MUST be stable for identical admissible inputs

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] Metadata Emission Specification — Node Identity Metadata
definitions.canonical_meaning: node identity MUST NOT depend on randomness, clock time, environment counters, renderer-local assignment, or incidental process state

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] Metadata Emission Specification — Metadata Admissibility Rules
definitions.canonical_meaning: mandatory values MUST NOT depend on absent fields, defaults, environment conventions, or renderer behavior

definitions.term: [MUST] Metadata Emission Specification — Metadata Admissibility Rules
definitions.canonical_meaning: all inter-field references within the payload MUST be internally consistent

Defines the canonical, minimal, machine-enforceable structural model for identity, organization boundaries, relationships, and scope anchors used as inputs to downstream constitutional systems.

This artifact defines structure only. It does not define permissions, legality, execution, workflow behavior, or presentation.
This schema SHALL not encode authority, behavior, execution logic, or evaluation rules.

definitions.term: [MUST] Organizational Schema System — Canonical Entity Types
definitions.canonical_meaning: each record MUST declare exactly one entity_type from this set

definitions.term: [MUST] Organizational Schema System — IV.1 Identity Record
definitions.canonical_meaning: all references to entities MUST resolve to a canonical identity record

definitions.term: [MUST] Organizational Schema System — VI.1 Relationship Record
definitions.canonical_meaning: cycles and topology constraints are not evaluated here but MUST be representable without ambiguity

definitions.term: [MUST] Organizational Schema System — VII.1 Membership Record
definitions.canonical_meaning: actor_ref MUST reference an actor identity

definitions.term: [MUST] Organizational Schema System — VIII.1 Resource Record
definitions.canonical_meaning: resource_id MUST be unique

definitions.term: [MUST] Organizational Schema System — VIII.2 Ownership Anchor
definitions.canonical_meaning: owner_entity_ref and owned_entity_ref MUST be valid identity_reference values

definitions.term: [SHALL NOT] Organizational Schema System — VIII.2 Ownership Anchor
definitions.canonical_meaning: ownership anchors SHALL NOT encode permissions or mutation rights

definitions.term: [MUST] Organizational Schema System — Replay and Version Binding
definitions.canonical_meaning: a complete set of records and versions sufficient to reconstruct identity and scope inputs MUST be available for replay
Projection execution MUST fail closed when deterministic derivation requirements are not satisfied.
input ordering MUST strictly follow CEK ordering guarantees

projection rebuild MUST produce identical output for identical lineage, identical projection specification, identical schema version, identical projection context where admitted, and identical IR bindings

definitions.term: [SHALL NOT] Untitled — Replay and IR Binding
definitions.canonical_meaning: projections SHALL NOT reinterpret, reorder, skip, filter, or supplement canonical replay inputs except where such behavior is explicitly declared in the projection specification as a lawful derivative operation over the full admitted source set
projection_spec_version MUST be explicit
projection lag MUST be externally observable where relevant to consuming systems
projections MUST NOT be treated as authoritative under staleness, freshness, completeness, or convenience
projection SHALL NOT autonomously initiate creation, invalidation, rebuild, activation, or retirement decisions
lifecycle decisions MUST originate in a separate governing artifact
absence of lifecycle governance in this document MUST NOT be interpreted as permission for implementation-local discretion over authoritative behavior

definitions.term: SiGMMa V2 - Prop Interface Contract Specification
definitions.canonical_meaning:
This specification defines the **Prop Interface Contract System** governing how generated React output invokes React Targets after `ComponentType` resolution has already been completed.

The UI Component Registry determines **which** React Target is invoked. This artifact determines **how** that target is invoked.

This artifact establishes a fully deterministic, schema-bound, non-optional invocation contract for every admissible generated component instance. It exists to eliminate component-side interpretation, implicit defaults, partial invocation, runtime repair, and prop-shape ambiguity.

This specification is mandatory for implementation readiness. Without a constitutionally defined prop interface contract, UI generation remains structurally incomplete because target resolution alone does not determine admissible invocation.

The Replay Semantics and Reconstruction Specification defines the authoritative rules by which the system reconstructs constitutional state from durable history.

It establishes:


Replay is not a convenience feature. It is the constitutional mechanism by which authoritative historical state is re-evaluated and reconstructed without reinterpretation.

The Runtime State Projection Boundary defines the only lawful boundary between authoritative runtime state and any projected representation derived from that state.

Its purpose is to prevent projected forms from acquiring authority they do not possess, to prevent interfaces and downstream systems from mutating constitutional meaning through presentation behavior, and to ensure that all derived views remain provably subordinate to authoritative state.

Runtime state is authoritative only where constitutional artifacts, command execution, adjudicated mutation, durable persistence, and replay-valid reconstruction establish it as such. Projection is never authority. Projection is an expression of already-authoritative state for consumption by interfaces, applications, operators, analytics, integrations, or other non-authoritative surfaces.

No projected form may redefine, complete, infer, repair, normalize, reinterpret, or otherwise replace runtime state. Projection is bounded expression only.

definitions.term: [MUST] Runtime State Projection Boundary Specification — Replay Classification
definitions.canonical_meaning: REPLAY_INFLUENTIAL projections MUST preserve sufficient context to explain decisions

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] Runtime State Projection Boundary Specification — Projection Confidence and Uncertainty Integrity
definitions.canonical_meaning: confidence levels MUST NOT be altered

definitions.term: [MUST] Runtime State Projection Boundary Specification — Projection-Induced Decision Risk Control
definitions.canonical_meaning: projection semantics MUST be sufficient to avoid misinterpretation

This constitution defines the hard system boundary between semantic processing and governed execution.

System pipeline:

Semantic Processing -> Compilation -> Command Proposal -> Command Submission -> Decision System -> CEK -> Projection

Rules:

This document defines the full constitutional structure of the SiGMMa V2 system as it exists in its completed and hardened state. It serves as the authoritative navigation map of all constitutional artifacts, their relationships, dependencies, and authority boundaries.

This document does not introduce new law. It reconciles and maps existing canonical artifacts.

definitions.term: [MUST] SiGMMa V2 — Constitutional Structure Document — A. CEK Layer (03_canonical/cek/)
definitions.canonical_meaning: Replay safety MUST be guaranteed by persistence layer ensuring identical state reconstruction

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] SiGMMa V2 — Constitutional Structure Document — B. System Layer (03_canonical/system/)
definitions.canonical_meaning: Workflow MUST NOT modify command payloads or execution outcomes

definitions.term: [MUST] SiGMMa V2 — Constitutional Structure Document — B. Invariant and Consistency System
definitions.canonical_meaning: All state transitions MUST be atomic

The State Mutation Record (SMR) is the authoritative, deterministic representation of all state transitions resulting from valid command execution within the system.

It is the canonical structure that:


No state change exists outside a State Mutation Record.

Defines machine-enforceable global invariants for the system layer and the conditions under which evaluation, mutation, commit, and replay are permitted or must terminate.

This artifact replaces descriptive audit with enforceable constraints.

definitions.term: [SHALL] System Invariant and Consistency Enforcement — III.1 Single Mutation Path
definitions.canonical_meaning: CEK SHALL reject and terminate

definitions.term: [SHALL] System Invariant and Consistency Enforcement — III.2 Authority Containment
definitions.canonical_meaning: CEK SHALL reject and terminate

definitions.term: [SHALL] System Invariant and Consistency Enforcement — III.3 Semantic Non-Authority
definitions.canonical_meaning: submission SHALL be rejected prior to CEK

definitions.term: [SHALL] System Invariant and Consistency Enforcement — III.4 Schema-Bound Execution
definitions.canonical_meaning: Decision System SHALL reject

definitions.term: [SHALL] System Invariant and Consistency Enforcement — III.5 IR-Bound Legality
definitions.canonical_meaning: CEK SHALL reject and terminate

definitions.term: [SHALL] System Invariant and Consistency Enforcement — III.6 Replay Determinism
definitions.canonical_meaning: replay SHALL fail

definitions.term: [SHALL] System Invariant and Consistency Enforcement — III.7 Organizational Boundary Integrity
definitions.canonical_meaning: Decision System SHALL reject

definitions.term: [SHALL] System Invariant and Consistency Enforcement — III.8 Projection Non-Authority
definitions.canonical_meaning: CEK SHALL reject

definitions.term: [SHALL] System Invariant and Consistency Enforcement — III.9 Workflow Non-Mutation
definitions.canonical_meaning: CEK SHALL reject

definitions.term: [SHALL] System Invariant and Consistency Enforcement — III.10 Typed Failure Closure
definitions.canonical_meaning: evaluation SHALL terminate

definitions.term: [SHALL] System Invariant and Consistency Enforcement — III.11 Non-Bypassability
definitions.canonical_meaning: CEK SHALL reject and terminate

definitions.term: [SHALL] System Invariant and Consistency Enforcement — III.12 Invariant Composability
definitions.canonical_meaning: system SHALL emit typed failure indicating composability violation

definitions.term: [MUST] System Invariant and Consistency Enforcement — Violation Handling
definitions.canonical_meaning: evaluation MUST terminate deterministically

definitions.term: SiGMMa V2 - Terminal Value Lexical Grammar
definitions.canonical_meaning:
This specification defines the **Terminal Value Lexical Grammar** governing the admissible lexical form of all primitive terminal values and token-resolved terminal values that appear in generated UI output.

This artifact closes the value-level determinism boundary of the UI generation system.

The registry defines target identity. The prop interface contract defines invocation structure. The metadata emission system defines traceability and recoverability. This artifact defines the admissible lexical form of terminal values once structural selection and invocation admissibility have already been established.

Without a governed terminal lexical grammar, structurally valid generation could still emit values that are ambiguous, multiply representable, normalization-dependent, locale-dependent, coercion-dependent, or renderer-dependent. That would break canonical serialization, replay comparability, audit fidelity, and deterministic output guarantees.

definitions.term: SiGMMa V2 - UI Component Registry Specification
definitions.canonical_meaning:
This specification defines the **UI Component Registry** as the authoritative system-layer artifact that binds each admissible UI IR `ComponentType` to exactly one admissible React generation target.

This artifact closes a mandatory system dependency required for:


The registry is not a convenience table, implementation preference list, package-local configuration file, or runtime selection mechanism. The registry is a constitutional binding system. Its function is to establish the final structural target identity that generation is permitted to invoke.

definitions.term: [MUST] UI IR Schema Definition — 0.3 UI Constitutional Doctrine
definitions.canonical_meaning: UI MUST render only what has been constitutionally and structurally defined

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] UI IR Schema Definition — 0.3 UI Constitutional Doctrine
definitions.canonical_meaning: UI MUST NOT compensate for missing definitions
`component_types` MUST contain every Component Type referenced anywhere in the package
`zones` MUST be non-empty

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] UI IR Schema Definition — 2.2 Zone
definitions.canonical_meaning: a Zone MUST NOT belong to more than one Surface
every component instance MUST belong to exactly one Zone through either direct root placement or transitive slot containment under a root component in that Zone

definitions.term: [MUST] UI IR Schema Definition — 2.3 Component Type
definitions.canonical_meaning: if a Component Type permits no slots, `slots` MUST be an explicit empty set

definitions.term: [MUST] UI IR Schema Definition — 2.4 Component Instance
definitions.canonical_meaning: `id` MUST be unique within the Surface scope

definitions.term: [MUST] UI IR Schema Definition — 2.5 Slot Definition
definitions.canonical_meaning: `allowed_types` MUST be explicit and MUST be non-empty unless a future artifact authorizes a deliberately uninhabitable slot class. No such class exists in v1

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] UI IR Schema Definition — 2.5 Slot Definition
definitions.canonical_meaning: if `ordering = NONE`, order MUST NOT be used to create meaning during compilation or rendering

definitions.term: [MUST] UI IR Schema Definition — 2.6 Slot Binding
definitions.canonical_meaning: child count MUST satisfy Slot Definition `cardinality`

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] UI IR Schema Definition — 2.6 Slot Binding
definitions.canonical_meaning: a child Component Instance MUST NOT appear in more than one SlotBinding across the same Surface

definitions.term: [MUST] UI IR Schema Definition — 2.8 State Binding
definitions.canonical_meaning: `state` MUST be a valid v1 StateType

definitions.term: [MUST NOT] UI IR Schema Definition — 2.8 State Binding
definitions.canonical_meaning: state absence MUST NOT be inferred as false unless a future artifact explicitly defines such semantics. No such semantics are defined in this artifact

definitions.term: [MUST] UI IR Schema Definition — 2.9 Token Binding
definitions.canonical_meaning: `token_id` MUST resolve uniquely

definitions.term: [MUST] UI IR Schema Definition — 3.3 Scope Rules
definitions.canonical_meaning: token identifiers MUST resolve within the token namespace exposed by the bound token system for the relevant `UIIRPackage`
`resolver_contract_id` MUST identify the deterministic resolution contract the compiler uses to validate token references and obtain terminal values or explicit failure
`resolver_contract` MUST be explicit
every referenced namespace MUST exist
`exact_match_only` MUST be true
Every workflow step emitting a command SHALL bind to exactly one IR Binding Reference via the Decision System.

definitions.term: [SHALL NOT] Workflow System Constitution — Core Invariants
definitions.canonical_meaning: Workflow SHALL NOT evaluate legality; legality is IR-bound and CEK-executed.

definitions.term: [MUST] Workflow System Constitution — Waiting and Resumption
definitions.canonical_meaning: all resumption inputs MUST be typed and admissible
It MUST be rejected OR
the system MUST surface decision instability

Define the complete system specification for safely extending SiGMMa and Organyz with reference-to-realization functionality. This specification covers how the system accepts under-specified user requests that reference dynamic, ambiguous, stylistic, perceptual, or non-canonical source phenomena, and how it transforms them into admissible, reproducible, bounded execution packages without allowing silent assumption injection.

This document defines the architecture, rules, data structures, control flow, enforcement boundaries, external knowledge usage model, realization integration model, validation model, and replay/audit requirements for this capability.

This document defines the governing doctrine for tiered engine service within SiGMMa V2.

It establishes how decision-relevant material may enter, be processed by, mature within, and eventually become authoritative inside the engine without compromising constitutional guarantees.

This doctrine resolves the architectural problem of broad intake under uneven assurance.

The engine must be able to process virtually all decision-relevant material in a given environment, but only material that satisfies the required assurance conditions may govern, authorize, or mutate canonical state.

The engine therefore operates under a progressive assurance model rather than a binary distinction between accepted and rejected material.
